title: ACL Injury
oneLiner: Rupture of anterior cruciate ligament causing knee instability; common in athletes; may lead to meniscal tears and osteoarthritis if mismanaged.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Knee dislocation (vascular emergency):** the single finding is **absent distal pulses** (popliteal artery injury) – do NOT rely on capillary refill; check ankle-brachial index (ABI) or Doppler.
- **Septic arthritis:** the single finding is **extreme pain on *passive* range of motion of the knee** (not just active) plus systemic upset (fever, rigors) – can mimic haemarthrosis but mandates urgent aspiration.
- **Acute compartment syndrome (rare with isolated ACL, but consider if multi-ligament trauma):** pain out of proportion, tense swelling, pain on passive stretch of toes.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Audible "pop" or tearing sensation at time of injury
  - Rapid knee swelling (haemarthrosis within 1-2 hours)
  - Knee gives way / feels unstable (especially with pivoting)
  - Pain and difficulty weight bearing immediately
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Significant effusion (ballotable patella)
  - Positive **Lachman test** (>5 mm anterior translation compared to normal knee)
  - Positive **anterior drawer test** (less sensitive than Lachman)
  - Positive **pivot shift test** (often difficult in acute setting due to pain)
  - Reduced range of motion (extension loss due to effusion)
  - Check for posterior sag (rules out PCL injury)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- Most likely -> less likely, with 1-line differentiators:
  - **ACL tear** – positive Lachman, haemarthrosis, instability.
  - **Meniscal tear** – joint line tenderness, locking/catching, normal Lachman but positive McMurray.
  - **MCL injury** – valgus stress pain, opening with stress testing, tenderness over medial knee.
  - **Patellar dislocation** – medial patellar tenderness, positive apprehension test, often spontaneously reduced.
  - **Tibial plateau fracture** – bony tenderness, inability to bear weight, haemarthrosis; X-ray shows fracture.
  - **PCL injury** – posterior drawer, posterior sag; clinical instability less common acutely.
- **Splitter:** **Lachman test** – if >5 mm anterior translation + hard endpoint -> ACL tear; if firm endpoint but patient in pain -> consider meniscal bucket-handle tear or simple haemarthrosis (e.g., from osteochondral fracture).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Distal neurovascular check (pulses, sensation, motor function)
  - Assess for effusion (ballottement)
  - Lachman and anterior drawer
- **Bloods:**
  - Not routine unless suspect septic arthritis (FBC, CRP, blood cultures) or poly-trauma (coag, group & save)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** X-ray knee (AP, lateral, and skyline or notch view) – rules out fracture (Segond fracture = pathognomonic for ACL tear; also look for tibial spine avulsion, patellar dislocation, osteochondral fragments)
  - **Second-line:** MRI knee (gold standard for ACL, meniscal, and cartilage injury); indicated if instability, persistent swelling, or if planning surgery
  - **CT:** not indicated unless tibial plateau fracture or multi-ligament knee dislocation (consider CT angiogram if pulse abnormal)
- **Special tests:**
  - Lachman (most sensitive for acute ACL)
  - Anterior drawer (specific but less sensitive)
  - Pivot shift (specific for rotational instability)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Lachman grading:**
    - Grade I: 3–5 mm translation (normal endpoint)
    - Grade II: 5–10 mm translation (soft endpoint)
    - Grade III: >10 mm translation (no endpoint)
  - **IKDC (International Knee Documentation Committee) score** for outcome; not used acutely.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **X-ray knee (AP + lateral)** – do not proceed to MRI without an X-ray; a missed tibial plateau fracture can be catastrophic if weight bearing continues. Also, check for patellar dislocation (often reduced at scene but may have loose bodies).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **RICE** (Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation) – ice pack for 20 min every 2 hours
  - **Crutches** – non-weight bearing or partial weight bearing if unable to walk
  - **Analgesia** – paracetamol 1 g PO QDS; consider NSAID (ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS) if no contraindications
  - **Immobilisation** – knee splint or brace in full extension (if comfortable) to reduce pain and swelling
  - **Aspiration** – consider large haemarthrosis with severe pain (in expert hands only; send fluid for culture if septic arthritis suspected)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Rarely admitted unless multi-ligament knee injury, open injury, or vascular concern
  - If admitted: continue RICE, crutches, analgesia; arrange MRI if not yet done
  - Physiotherapy: gentle quadriceps sets, passive range of motion as tolerated (avoid active extension against resistance)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Conservative management** (first-line for most): physiotherapy programme focusing on quadriceps strengthening, neuromuscular training, and proprioception. Use of hinged knee brace for high-demand activities.
  - **Surgical reconstruction** indicated for:
    - Significant functional instability (giving way episodes)
    - High-demand athletes (pivoting sports)
    - Combined ACL + meniscal tear (especially in young patients)
    - Failed conservative management
  - Timing: surgery usually delayed 3–6 weeks to allow swelling to settle and regain full range of motion
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Not typical; if admitted for multi-ligament injury: nil by mouth if theatre planned, IV fluids (Hartmann’s 1 L/8h if NBM), VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily if >4 hours immobile), neurovascular observations 4-hourly.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Paracetamol** 1 g PO QDS (max 4 g/day) – safe, no dose adjustment in renal/hepatic impairment unless severe (reduce dose to 500 mg QDS if eGFR <30 or cirrhosis)
  - **Ibuprofen** 400–600 mg PO TDS (max 1.2 g/day) – for 5-7 days; with PPI (omeprazole 20 mg PO daily) if >65 or history of dyspepsia
  - **Naproxen** 250–500 mg PO BD – alternative to ibuprofen
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Codeine phosphate** 30–60 mg PO QDS (add to paracetamol if pain not controlled; note constipation risk)
  - **Tramadol** 50–100 mg PO QDS (CNS depression risk; avoid in elderly or on SSRIs)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid if active peptic ulcer, severe asthma, CKD (eGFR <30)
  - Tramadol: avoid with SSRIs/SNRIs (serotonin syndrome risk)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR <30; caution if eGFR 30-60
  - Paracetamol: reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment (max 2 g/day) and severe renal impairment (eGFR <30)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **NSAID overdose in CKD** – prescribe PPI and check renal function before repeating course; avoid in dehydration or elderly.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Haemarthrosis (may cause quadriceps inhibition)
  - Meniscal tear (especially lateral meniscus in acute injury)
  - Associated ligament injuries (MCL, PCL, posterolateral corner) – missed if not examined
  - Patellofemoral pain from altered gait
- **Chronic:**
  - Persistent knee instability (giving way, pivot shift)
  - Secondary meniscal tears (medial meniscus more common in chronic ACL deficiency)
  - Osteoarthritis (cartilage damage from instability and recurrent injury)
  - Quadriceps atrophy (from disuse or incomplete rehab)
sections.Prognosis:
- With appropriate physiotherapy, ~50% of non-surgically managed patients achieve satisfactory function and return to sports (often with activity modification).
- After ACL reconstruction, ~80% return to prior level of sport, but re-rupture rate is ~5-10% (higher in young, active, or return to sport <9 months).
- Without treatment, risk of meniscal tear and osteoarthritis increases significantly over 10-20 years.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Perform the Lachman test** – it is the most reliable clinical test for acute ACL injury, even with haemarthrosis.
- **Always X-ray the knee** before MRI to rule out fractures (Segond, tibial spine avulsion) – a fracture changes management and may need urgent orthopaedic input.
- **Refer to physiotherapy early** – even if planning surgery, pre-operative quadriceps strength improves surgical outcomes.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not assume all knee effusions are traumatic** – in a febrile patient or with excruciating passive motion, consider septic arthritis and arrange urgent aspiration.
- **Do not miss posterior drawer sign** – an ACL-deficient knee with a false-positive anterior drawer can be a PCL injury; look for posterior sag of the tibia.
- **Do not schedule ACL reconstruction too early** – wait until haemarthrosis resolves and full range of motion is regained (usually 3-6 weeks) to reduce risk of arthrofibrosis.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Severe pain not controlled with simple analgesia
  - Inability to bear weight after 72 hours
  - Mechanical locking or catching of the knee (suggests bucket-handle meniscal tear)
  - Signs of infection (fever, redness, warmth, swelling increasing after 48 hours)
  - Numbness or weakness in the leg
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP or orthopaedic clinic in 2 weeks for review of MRI results and referral to physiotherapy
  - If high-demand athlete or instability persists, refer to orthopaedic surgeon
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / orthopaedic team if:**
  - Absent distal pulses or ABI <0.9 (knee dislocation) – **urgent vascular surgery referral**
  - Signs of acute compartment syndrome (tense swelling, pain on passive stretch) – **call ortho reg immediately**; consider fasciotomy
  - Suspected septic arthritis (hot, swollen, febrile, painful passive motion) – **urgent ortho review for aspiration**
  - Open knee injury or neurovascular deficit
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L (especially in multi-trauma) – escalate to senior
  - GCS <15 or deteriorating consciousness – think head injury (if mechanism suggests)

rawMarkdown:
## ACL Injury

**One-liner:** Rupture of anterior cruciate ligament causing knee instability; common in athletes; may lead to meniscal tears and osteoarthritis if mismanaged.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Knee dislocation (vascular emergency):** the single finding is **absent distal pulses** (popliteal artery injury) – do NOT rely on capillary refill; check ankle-brachial index (ABI) or Doppler.
- **Septic arthritis:** the single finding is **extreme pain on *passive* range of motion of the knee** (not just active) plus systemic upset (fever, rigors) – can mimic haemarthrosis but mandates urgent aspiration.
- **Acute compartment syndrome (rare with isolated ACL, but consider if multi-ligament trauma):** pain out of proportion, tense swelling, pain on passive stretch of toes.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Audible "pop" or tearing sensation at time of injury
  - Rapid knee swelling (haemarthrosis within 1-2 hours)
  - Knee gives way / feels unstable (especially with pivoting)
  - Pain and difficulty weight bearing immediately
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Significant effusion (ballotable patella)
  - Positive **Lachman test** (>5 mm anterior translation compared to normal knee)
  - Positive **anterior drawer test** (less sensitive than Lachman)
  - Positive **pivot shift test** (often difficult in acute setting due to pain)
  - Reduced range of motion (extension loss due to effusion)
  - Check for posterior sag (rules out PCL injury)

### Differential Diagnosis
- Most likely -> less likely, with 1-line differentiators:
  - **ACL tear** – positive Lachman, haemarthrosis, instability.
  - **Meniscal tear** – joint line tenderness, locking/catching, normal Lachman but positive McMurray.
  - **MCL injury** – valgus stress pain, opening with stress testing, tenderness over medial knee.
  - **Patellar dislocation** – medial patellar tenderness, positive apprehension test, often spontaneously reduced.
  - **Tibial plateau fracture** – bony tenderness, inability to bear weight, haemarthrosis; X-ray shows fracture.
  - **PCL injury** – posterior drawer, posterior sag; clinical instability less common acutely.
- **Splitter:** **Lachman test** – if >5 mm anterior translation + hard endpoint -> ACL tear; if firm endpoint but patient in pain -> consider meniscal bucket-handle tear or simple haemarthrosis (e.g., from osteochondral fracture).

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Distal neurovascular check (pulses, sensation, motor function)
  - Assess for effusion (ballottement)
  - Lachman and anterior drawer
- **Bloods:**
  - Not routine unless suspect septic arthritis (FBC, CRP, blood cultures) or poly-trauma (coag, group & save)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** X-ray knee (AP, lateral, and skyline or notch view) – rules out fracture (Segond fracture = pathognomonic for ACL tear; also look for tibial spine avulsion, patellar dislocation, osteochondral fragments)
  - **Second-line:** MRI knee (gold standard for ACL, meniscal, and cartilage injury); indicated if instability, persistent swelling, or if planning surgery
  - **CT:** not indicated unless tibial plateau fracture or multi-ligament knee dislocation (consider CT angiogram if pulse abnormal)
- **Special tests:**
  - Lachman (most sensitive for acute ACL)
  - Anterior drawer (specific but less sensitive)
  - Pivot shift (specific for rotational instability)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Lachman grading:**
    - Grade I: 3–5 mm translation (normal endpoint)
    - Grade II: 5–10 mm translation (soft endpoint)
    - Grade III: >10 mm translation (no endpoint)
  - **IKDC (International Knee Documentation Committee) score** for outcome; not used acutely.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **X-ray knee (AP + lateral)** – do not proceed to MRI without an X-ray; a missed tibial plateau fracture can be catastrophic if weight bearing continues. Also, check for patellar dislocation (often reduced at scene but may have loose bodies).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **RICE** (Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation) – ice pack for 20 min every 2 hours
  - **Crutches** – non-weight bearing or partial weight bearing if unable to walk
  - **Analgesia** – paracetamol 1 g PO QDS; consider NSAID (ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS) if no contraindications
  - **Immobilisation** – knee splint or brace in full extension (if comfortable) to reduce pain and swelling
  - **Aspiration** – consider large haemarthrosis with severe pain (in expert hands only; send fluid for culture if septic arthritis suspected)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Rarely admitted unless multi-ligament knee injury, open injury, or vascular concern
  - If admitted: continue RICE, crutches, analgesia; arrange MRI if not yet done
  - Physiotherapy: gentle quadriceps sets, passive range of motion as tolerated (avoid active extension against resistance)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Conservative management** (first-line for most): physiotherapy programme focusing on quadriceps strengthening, neuromuscular training, and proprioception. Use of hinged knee brace for high-demand activities.
  - **Surgical reconstruction** indicated for:
    - Significant functional instability (giving way episodes)
    - High-demand athletes (pivoting sports)
    - Combined ACL + meniscal tear (especially in young patients)
    - Failed conservative management
  - Timing: surgery usually delayed 3–6 weeks to allow swelling to settle and regain full range of motion
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Not typical; if admitted for multi-ligament injury: nil by mouth if theatre planned, IV fluids (Hartmann’s 1 L/8h if NBM), VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily if >4 hours immobile), neurovascular observations 4-hourly.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Paracetamol** 1 g PO QDS (max 4 g/day) – safe, no dose adjustment in renal/hepatic impairment unless severe (reduce dose to 500 mg QDS if eGFR <30 or cirrhosis)
  - **Ibuprofen** 400–600 mg PO TDS (max 1.2 g/day) – for 5-7 days; with PPI (omeprazole 20 mg PO daily) if >65 or history of dyspepsia
  - **Naproxen** 250–500 mg PO BD – alternative to ibuprofen
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Codeine phosphate** 30–60 mg PO QDS (add to paracetamol if pain not controlled; note constipation risk)
  - **Tramadol** 50–100 mg PO QDS (CNS depression risk; avoid in elderly or on SSRIs)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid if active peptic ulcer, severe asthma, CKD (eGFR <30)
  - Tramadol: avoid with SSRIs/SNRIs (serotonin syndrome risk)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR <30; caution if eGFR 30-60
  - Paracetamol: reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment (max 2 g/day) and severe renal impairment (eGFR <30)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **NSAID overdose in CKD** – prescribe PPI and check renal function before repeating course; avoid in dehydration or elderly.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Haemarthrosis (may cause quadriceps inhibition)
  - Meniscal tear (especially lateral meniscus in acute injury)
  - Associated ligament injuries (MCL, PCL, posterolateral corner) – missed if not examined
  - Patellofemoral pain from altered gait
- **Chronic:**
  - Persistent knee instability (giving way, pivot shift)
  - Secondary meniscal tears (medial meniscus more common in chronic ACL deficiency)
  - Osteoarthritis (cartilage damage from instability and recurrent injury)
  - Quadriceps atrophy (from disuse or incomplete rehab)

### Prognosis
- With appropriate physiotherapy, ~50% of non-surgically managed patients achieve satisfactory function and return to sports (often with activity modification).
- After ACL reconstruction, ~80% return to prior level of sport, but re-rupture rate is ~5-10% (higher in young, active, or return to sport <9 months).
- Without treatment, risk of meniscal tear and osteoarthritis increases significantly over 10-20 years.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Perform the Lachman test** – it is the most reliable clinical test for acute ACL injury, even with haemarthrosis.
- **Always X-ray the knee** before MRI to rule out fractures (Segond, tibial spine avulsion) – a fracture changes management and may need urgent orthopaedic input.
- **Refer to physiotherapy early** – even if planning surgery, pre-operative quadriceps strength improves surgical outcomes.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not assume all knee effusions are traumatic** – in a febrile patient or with excruciating passive motion, consider septic arthritis and arrange urgent aspiration.
- **Do not miss posterior drawer sign** – an ACL-deficient knee with a false-positive anterior drawer can be a PCL injury; look for posterior sag of the tibia.
- **Do not schedule ACL reconstruction too early** – wait until haemarthrosis resolves and full range of motion is regained (usually 3-6 weeks) to reduce risk of arthrofibrosis.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Severe pain not controlled with simple analgesia
  - Inability to bear weight after 72 hours
  - Mechanical locking or catching of the knee (suggests bucket-handle meniscal tear)
  - Signs of infection (fever, redness, warmth, swelling increasing after 48 hours)
  - Numbness or weakness in the leg
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP or orthopaedic clinic in 2 weeks for review of MRI results and referral to physiotherapy
  - If high-demand athlete or instability persists, refer to orthopaedic surgeon

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / orthopaedic team if:**
  - Absent distal pulses or ABI <0.9 (knee dislocation) – **urgent vascular surgery referral**
  - Signs of acute compartment syndrome (tense swelling, pain on passive stretch) – **call ortho reg immediately**; consider fasciotomy
  - Suspected septic arthritis (hot, swollen, febrile, painful passive motion) – **urgent ortho review for aspiration**
  - Open knee injury or neurovascular deficit
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L (especially in multi-trauma) – escalate to senior
  - GCS <15 or deteriorating consciousness – think head injury (if mechanism suggests)
